title: The Cache Everything Trap
short_description: An over-cached Spark pipeline is slower because memory is filled with DataFrames that are barely reused.
business_context: A nightly feature-engineering job used to finish comfortably before the morning SLA. Over time, engineers kept adding `.cache()` calls defensively, and now the cluster spills heavily and the job runs slower despite extra memory.
problem_statement: Review the code snippet and the storage metrics. Explain which caches are unnecessary, what should be unpersisted, and why over-caching is hurting the job.
student_task: Write a debugging answer that identifies the unnecessary cache usage and proposes a safer cache and unpersist strategy.
learning_goals: Cache only DataFrames that are expensive and genuinely reused.
Understand the trade-off between cache convenience and memory pressure.
Recognize when `unpersist()` should be part of the lifecycle.
broken_code:
base = spark.read.parquet(raw_path).cache()
clean = transform_base(base).cache()
enriched = enrich(clean, dim_customers).cache()
scored = score(enriched).cache()
final = publish(scored)
final.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
production_logs: 2026-06-11 01:31:20 WARN executor memory spill increased 4.2x week-over-week
2026-06-11 01:31:20 WARN cached DataFrames remain resident after single downstream use
solution_query:
Model answer:
- Most of the pipeline is cached even though `base`, `clean`, and `scored` are each consumed only once.
- Those one-time DataFrames should not be cached because they occupy memory without offsetting recomputation cost.
- If `enriched` is truly reused in multiple expensive downstream branches, it is the only strong caching candidate in the sample metrics.
- Add explicit `unpersist()` once a cached DataFrame is no longer needed so long-lived jobs do not retain stale memory pressure.
explanation: Caching is not free. Every cached DataFrame competes for memory, and caching one-time intermediates can make the cluster slower through spill and eviction churn. The right pattern is selective caching plus cleanup, not blanket persistence everywhere.
common_mistakes: Treating every transformation stage as a caching candidate by default.
Ignoring `reuse_count` and cached size when deciding what to persist.
Forgetting to unpersist expensive intermediates after the last downstream consumer.

rubric.point: Identifies that most cached DataFrames are only used once and should not be cached.
Calls out memory spill or eviction churn as the operational consequence.
Selects only truly reused expensive DataFrames as cache candidates.
Mentions unpersisting cached data when it is no longer needed.

hints: Caching pays off only if recomputation cost is avoided more than once.
The metrics table already tells you which intermediates are barely reused.
A good answer covers both what to stop caching and what to unpersist.
